cards.front: What is FHRP and why do we need it?
cards.back: First Hop Redundancy Protocol. Lets a group of routers share a single virtual IP + virtual MAC, so hosts can keep one default gateway. If active router fails, another takes over the virtual IP/MAC — clients never notice.

1. **HSRP** — Cisco-proprietary, most common in Cisco networks
2. **VRRP** — IETF open standard (RFC 5798)
3. **GLBP** — Cisco-proprietary, load-balances across multiple active routers

cards.front:
HSRP virtual MAC format: [...]`XX` (XX = group # in hex)
HSRP group 25 vMAC = 0000.0c07.ac19 (25 dec = 19 hex)

HSRP virtual MAC format: [...]`XX` (XX = group # in hex)
HSRP group 25 vMAC = 0000.0c07.ac19 (25 dec = 19 hex)
GLBP virtual MAC format: [...] (XX = group, YY = AVF index 01-04)

cards.back:
- Priority = ==100== (range 0-255, higher wins)
- Preempt = **OFF** by default
- Hello timer = 3s
- Hold time = 10s

- Priority = ==100== (range 1-254; 255 means IP owner)
- Preempt = **ON** by default
- Hello (Advertisement) = 1s
- Hold time = 3s (master_down_interval = 3*hello + skew)

cards.front: GLBP defaults — priority + preempt + load balance:
cards.back:
- Priority = ==100==
- Preempt = **OFF** by default
- Load balance: **round-robin** (alternatives: weighted, host-dependent)
- Hello = 3s, Hold = 10s

1. Highest priority wins → **Active**
2. Tie → highest IP address
3. **Non-preemptive by default** — higher priority joining later does NOT take over

cards.front: Configure basic HSRP on an interface:
cards.back:
```
interface g0/0
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.0
 standby 1 ip 10.0.0.254              ! virtual IP
 standby 1 priority 110
 standby 1 preempt                    ! enable preemption
 standby 1 name PROD-GW
```

```
interface g0/0
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.0
 vrrp 1 ip 10.0.0.254
 vrrp 1 priority 110
 vrrp 1 preempt                       ! actually ON by default
```

```
interface g0/0
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.0
 glbp 1 ip 10.0.0.254
 glbp 1 priority 110
 glbp 1 preempt
 glbp 1 load-balancing round-robin   ! or weighted, host-dependent
```

cards.front: HSRP **interface tracking** — what does it do?
cards.back:
Decrement priority if a tracked interface goes down (e.g. uplink to ISP fails). Lets a secondary router take over even if its LAN-side interface is still up.
```
standby 1 track g0/1 decrement 20
```
If g0/1 down → priority drops 110 → 90 → loses election.

Interface tracking watches link state only. Object tracking can watch:
- IP route in the table
- IP SLA reachability test results
- Multiple interfaces (boolean AND/OR)
- More flexible:
```
track 10 ip route 8.8.8.0/24 reachability
standby 1 track 10 decrement 20
```

HSRP 6 states from boot to active: Initial → Learn (no vIP yet) → Listen (heard hellos) → Speak (sending hellos, contesting) → Standby (2nd highest priority) → Active (forwards traffic, owns vMAC). Active sends hello every 3s, holdtime 10s. Group 0-255 (v1) or 0-4095 (v2). vMAC: 0000.0c07.acXX.

cards.front: What does the Active router actually do?
cards.back: Receives packets sent to the **virtual MAC** (which clients resolved from ARP for virtual IP), forwards them. All other group members do nothing for client traffic.

```
show standby                          ! all groups, all interfaces
show standby brief                    ! one-line per group: Pri, P (preempt), State, Active, Standby, Virtual IP
show standby g0/0                     ! one interface
```

cards.front: In `show standby brief`, what do the columns mean?
cards.back:
- **Grp** — group number
- **Pri** — this router's priority
- **P** — preempt enabled? (P or blank)
- **State** — Active / Standby / Listen / Speak / Init
- **Active addr** — IP of current active router
- **Standby addr** — IP of current standby
- **Virtual IP** — the gateway IP clients use

cards.front: GLBP unique feature — what makes it different from HSRP/VRRP?
cards.back: **Multiple active forwarders per group**. Up to 4 routers actively forward traffic (each gets a different vMAC). One AVG (Active Virtual Gateway) replies to ARPs, distributing client requests across the 4 AVFs (Active Virtual Forwarders). True load balancing, not just redundancy.

- **AVG** = Active Virtual Gateway — single router per group, replies to client ARP for vIP. Hands out different vMACs (load distribution).
- **AVF** = Active Virtual Forwarder — actually forwards traffic for one vMAC. Up to 4 AVFs per group.

cards.front: When would you use VRRP over HSRP?
cards.back: Multi-vendor environment (Cisco + non-Cisco gear). VRRP is an IETF standard, supported across vendors. HSRP locks you into Cisco.

cards.front: When would GLBP shine over HSRP?
cards.back: When you want **load balancing**, not just redundancy. Two upstream links, two routers — GLBP uses both simultaneously. HSRP/VRRP only uses the Active router; Standby sits idle until failover.

```
show vrrp brief
show vrrp interface g0/0
show glbp brief
show glbp g0/0
```
